speaker: interviewer
text: October the 23rd, 1996. The survivor is Sally, maiden name Zielinska, Roisman. Jeff Lewis, interviewer. It's in California, LA, USA, to be in English.

speaker: interviewer
text: Today's date is October 23, 1996. I'm interviewing Sally Roisman in Los Angeles, California. My name is Jeff Lewis. Good afternoon.

speaker: interviewee
text: Good afternoon.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you please tell me your name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sally Roisman.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is your maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Zielinska, Zielinsky in German.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were you born, Sally?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in Sosnowiec, Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born October 2, 1930.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old are you today?

speaker: interviewee
text: I am 66 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sally, tell me a little bit about your family when you were growing up.

speaker: interviewee
text: I am one of 11 children. My-- I was born to a strictly Orthodox family. My father had a very long beard, very pious person.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewer
text: Shaia Berish. Who prayed three times a day, morning and [HEBREW], towards the sun down. We were middle class people. My father was a wholesale-- textile wholesaler. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We lived a good life prior to the war. There was a lot of love and family.

speaker: interviewer
text: When one of the children was sick, my mother said, 'What difference does it make which finger hurts me?' Very devoted. What was your mother's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hinda.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was her maiden name? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: Perczyk.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you like to name your brothers and sisters before we get too far into this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. My oldest brother was Hersh Mayer. Second one was Eliezar. The third born was my sister, Esther. Then was Itzak, Simon. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Then was Mary, Nathan, I, younger brother Abraham, younger sister Roizula, and the youngest Yentela.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me a little bit about what a Sabbath dinner was like with all of your brothers and sisters and your parents.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sabbath started at Friday afternoon. Friday, my mother was baking and cooking most of the day because we did not bake or cook during Shabbos. And it was a very happy occasion. Nobody worked. My-- my father at the head of the table. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Food was being consumed. And after the meals, my father said the prayers with my older brothers. And the-- the children sat at a smaller table. And it was just a great feeling being a part of that family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a favorite holiday that you celebrated?

speaker: interviewee
text: Our favorite holiday must have been for the children Sukkot since we got to prepare and decorate the sukkah. But there were some other holidays. Shavuot was happy. And Passover was a very happy holiday for us. My father would sit-- lean-- lean back, as-- and we had beautiful [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] glasses, special glasses for our children, which were very colorful. And we-- we had to drink seven [HEBREW] at the seder. And the little ones fell asleep.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever find the afikoman?

speaker: interviewee
text: I did. It was very exciting, searching, looking for the afikoman. My brothers were ahead of me, as usual.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me a little bit about the school that you went to.

speaker: interviewee
text: I attended a school-- a Jewish girls school, [POLISH]. But unfortunately I didn't get to have a lot of education prior to the war because when the Germans marched into Poland in 1939, our school was closed. And since then, we had no education.

speaker: interviewer
text: Moving back a little before the Germans marched in, do you-- did you have favorite subjects that you liked to study in school, or-- was there something you were good at?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- in fact, I liked math very much. And when my mother would go to talk to our teachers, and when she'd come home, she was full of praise and happiness. I-- I feel good that I made my mother happy. And also the fact that I was a very good student, my teacher called me [SPEAKING POLISH]. That's my little [POLISH]. And it gave me good feeling for the rest of my life. And I resumed my studies after the war since my studies were interrupted, had no school for five years. And even now I continue studying. I call myself a perpetual student. Took a lot of courses. Got my high school-- in-- back in Germany. I had private tutors coming into the house, and I wanted to become a dentist.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before we get to your career aspirations at-- after the war. Let me stay with Poland before the war. Tell me about your friends. Did you have many friends that you-- from school or from the neighborhood?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had a lot of friends from school, and-- Jewish friends. Also had Christian friend. Christian girlfriend who lived, in fact, in the same building. And-- and we played in the backyard together. And my brothers, unfortunately, didn't have close friends, only Jewish friends since they wore payos. And they were-- but the girls just mixed with the Christian population.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me a little bit about the apartment building in which you lived. How many apartments were there? How many families lived there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think there may have been 15 families or so. And in the apartment building where we lived, there was a synagogue, kind of a shtiebel that they called where my father used to go to pray three times a day.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many of the families that lived in your building were Jewish? Do you-- can you estimate that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm not quite sure. Maybe 50% or so.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever, when you were growing up, notice antisemitism? Or did your family ever talk about experiencing antisemitism?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we have experienced a lot of antisemitism. In fact, I personally experienced antisemitism walking to school. I had about 20 minutes to walk to school. Many a time, I had to watch to see where there were Christian children. If they were holding a stone in their hand, I would kind of hide not to be hit with-- but I did get hit with stones a couple of times.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were your brothers--

speaker: interviewee
text: But I--

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm sorry. Were your brothers and sisters attacked as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: During the war, my father was attacked. And he came home with blood running from his head. And we were just very upset. And we called the police. And we notified them of the incident. And they told us, they didn't kill him yet.

speaker: interviewer
text: So prior to the-- again, prior to the war, before the war, you-- there were-- you did see the antisemitism and--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I was aware there was antisemitism. But I also had Christian friends who were good-- good people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me what happened in 1939 and how it affected you.

speaker: interviewee
text: 1939 changed my life and my family's life completely. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We heard that the German army was going to march into Poland, and we fled. My family fled. Since we lived near the German border, we thought that we would be the first ones to be occupied. And my family fled on a horse-- on a wagon driven by horses deeper into Poland where my father was born in [POLISH]. On the way, we were attacked by Polacks. And they robbed us of some of the possessions which we were going to take with us. And when we stopped, I think it was [POLISH]. I'm not quite sure of the name. We heard that the Germans were all over Poland, and my parents decided to go back to where we came from, Sosnowiec. As we got back, we found that the janitor who was taking care of the building moved the entire building. And so we called the police, and they got him out and we moved back into our dwelling.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you keep up with the news at that time? Was there-- were there radios? Were  you still allowed to have radios at that time, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't have a radio. I really am not quite sure about that since I was nine years of age. I'm not quite sure where any news came from.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you continue to go to school?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. They closed our school and we stayed home.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what other things were you not allowed to do?

speaker: interviewee
text: At first, we were separated. We had to buy food at different times than the Polacks did. We also-- the older people had to wear yellow armbands with the Star of David. I didn't have to wear that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when my family was exchanging clothing for food from Christians, I would go to streets. We-- streets were restricted for Jews. Some streets were restricted. So they-- I was sent to a Christian shop to get food in exchange for clothing. And I was very, very afraid that I shouldn't be discovered that I was Jewish. But somehow I was never caught. Tell me what kind of food you were able to acquire at that time.

speaker: interviewee
text: Basic foods, bread. Since the portions that we were given were inadequate. So my family substituted bread, potatoes, carrots, and things like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there-- did you ever have to steal food?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't-- there was no opportunity of stealing food. But I did steal food in camp later on. I used to take out food from Camp Graben where I was from 1945-- from 1943 to 1945.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned that your brothers wore payos. Were they-- did they find themselves more mistreated, or your father because of his beard, or-- you know, because of their looks all of a sudden the antisemitism more flagrant?

speaker: interviewee
text: My-- my brothers' movements as well as my father's movements were very much restricted. Since my father had a long beard. And we witnessed ourselves that the Germans would tear out the beards by the roots, and so my father decided at first when he would leave the house, he would hide his beard in the coat which was-- he was wearing. And later on, he had to cut his beard in order not to get attacked by Germans. So that he looked liked a changed person. We hardly recognized him when his beard-- when he decided to cut his beard. Not completely. He left about two or three inches.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how--

speaker: interviewee
text: But he was a completely changed person.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long was his beard before?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was almost to his waist.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your father able to do any work at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father then had to give up work. And the older brothers took over the support of the family. He would very rarely leave. He was like a beaten person completely. It was-- his dignity and his honor was taken away from him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you all still able to observe religious events?

speaker: interviewee
text: We continued observing religious holidays. We continued having family meals with whatever there was on the table. There was substitute fish, which my mother made. And life went on, but at a completely changed pace, completely changed atmosphere at home. There was a lot of suffering and crying on my mother's part. And she would tell us that she feels so sorry that she cannot feed us the way she would like to feed us, that her cat-- she was-- she used to give her cat better food when she was single than she could give us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you able to play with other children? Did you have any normalcy in your life at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Our lives have changed. Our lives were just completely turned upside down.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the next major event? What was-- what was the first--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the major event actually was when the Germans marched into our city, the major event that stands out in my mind is the burning of the synagogue.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: They told us to stay inside, not to go out. They also gathered a few men in Sosnowiec, and they shot them on the spot while burning the synagogue. And a few days later, we went out. And it was a horrible sight, seeing the bricks and all the-- the Torahs burned, and the siddurs burned. It was a dramatic and a shocking, a tragic experience for me personally.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, this wasn't the synagogue that-- or the--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: --shul in the back or your-- this was a different--

speaker: interviewee
text: That-- that was the main synagogue, a large synagogue.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was the Jewish community able to still practice religion at the home after that event as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: At home we were able to practice religion.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me a little bit about your thoughts the first time you actually saw a Nazi. Do you remember that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember a Nazi marching, just stomping with their boots on the sidewalk. I remember the black shirts of the SS. And there were some who I think wore a red shirt. The black and red stands out in my mind. But I was very afraid. When I saw a-- a German soldier from a distance, I would cross the street or hide someplace. I was afraid of being hit, or just purely very much afraid of Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your brothers and sisters as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sally, how long did this environment stay the same?

speaker: interviewee
text: The environment stayed the same until they resettled us to a certain street where they-- there were Jews only living in that street.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the name of the street?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was Dekerta. Dekerta.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- and is this still in-- in Sosnowiec?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Sosnowiec. And we were told that a family that used to live there prior to our moving into that place was deported.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many families moved into this dwelling?

speaker: interviewee
text: At that time, it was [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] our-- our family. But my father was missing. I forgot to mention that there was a-- prior to moving into that new place, we were gathered into a large open area. And we were told that we have to get there a certain time in the morning. And the entire Sosnowiec was gathered at-- on-- in that area. And the Germans were-- we would walk by the Germans. And he was holding a kind of a wooden stick. And he would say, left-- to the left or right. My mother, the rest of the children, were ordered to go to one side, and my father to the other side. And we were then allowed-- we were told to go home. But they detained my father.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what happened? Did he--

speaker: interviewee
text: We were told that they were sent some place. We didn't know where. But that-- at that time, the real tragedy started since we lost the head of the house. Father was considered the head of the house. And my family-- my-- the lot of the entire family has changed. There was tragic sadness. And my-- when I would wake up at night, my mother would cry. And it's still ringing in my ears, her cry. And when I have-- you know, think back of these times, it was a tragic occurrence that happened. That was the first real tragedy that hit my family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever hear from your father again? Did you know what happened to him?

speaker: interviewee
text: We never heard from my father again. We heard rumors. And a few months later, my sister contacted her friends who had a-- an SS friend. She was a single lady, a Christian lady, who said that she is going to find out where they sent our father. We did a lot of favors to this lady. We gave her jewelry. And she and her SS friend set out to find out where they sent my father. And after a while, she came back. She was a sick person. Now, I was little. I was listening to what my sister had to say. And probably I suspect that my mother didn't want us to know, but it was a very sad answer. We kind of thought that our father is not alive anymore, from what she told us. She went to Auschwitz, and she saw what was going on there, but could not get herself to tell us the real story.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was the SS friend who went to Auschwitz--

speaker: interviewee
text: That was-- yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --and saw what was going on?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. She was a lady friend who had an SS man for a friend.

speaker: interviewer
text: And after your father was taken away, how did your family provide food for itself, and--

speaker: interviewee
text: At-- at that time, there was no monetary gain for work. We would just-- stayed home and we were given rations of food. And there was some clothing and jewelry exchange for food.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what kind of food you were give-- or rations you were given?

speaker: interviewee
text: Small portions of bread. I-- I really don't recall. But we all lost weight. And we were just constantly hungry.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did that-- did this continue, then? What-- what year are we in now, that your father was taken away?

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what happened after that-- after-- after that?

speaker: interviewee
text: After that, they resettled most of the Jews to just several streets. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it-- was it a physical ghetto? Did they put up fences, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Excuse me.

speaker: interviewee
text: The physical ghetto came later, in '43. I think it was January, '43, where they took us to a guarded ghetto. It was a very large open space, and there were guards there. And we were assigned about 14 people to a small room. At that stage, it wasn't just my family. We shared a room with other families.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any of those families?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't recall the names.

speaker: interviewer
text: How big they were?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't recall the names at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember how many people were in the room?

speaker: interviewer
text: Your family and--

speaker: interviewee
text: And more-- more people. Now at that stage, they took away two of my brothers to DP camps.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which-- which brothers?

speaker: interviewee
text: They took away Simon. They took Eliezar. And Itzak. It's three. They took away three brothers. At one time, we-- my brother, Eliezar, used to write very bad letters from a DP-- from a concentration camp. And the words were that he cannot survive. And he tried to ask us to get him back home. My sister, who was perhaps 17 years of age now, went to Moniek Merin, who was the head of the Jewish Judenrat. The Germans assigned Jews to rule over Jews. And somehow, it was like a miracle happened, that they sent my brother home. Eliezar was sent home. Little did we know then that it was a bad decision. Had he stayed in camp, he would have had chances of survival. But the fact that he came home, he was later taken away to Auschwitz or Birkenau. We're not sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know the name of the DP camps that your brothers were taken to?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember Simon's DP camp was [GERMAN]. And the other brothers, I-- I'm-- I don't recall the name. But after the end of the war, we were reunited with Nathan and Itzak in Buchenwald.

speaker: interviewer
text: So again, before we're liberated from Buchenwald, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] you're-- there are 14 people in a-- in this ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were-- what-- what was your mother doing at this time to keep everyone together? Were you still practicing your religion?

speaker: interviewee
text: We still kept on practicing religion. Without a father, it was different, completely different. And naturally, the Sabbath was still observed. And candles were lit. And my mother was a very, very brave lady. And she tried to keep the family the way-- the best way she could. Later on, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my sisters had to work, they-- two of my sisters. They were taken to work in Sosnowiec sewing uniforms for the Germans. Now, I didn't have to go to work. And one day, they didn't come home from work. And we were told that they were taken to a concentration camp from work.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know which camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: At the time, we didn't have any news from the camp. But a few weeks later, the Gestapo or S-- SS men was knocking very hard on our door in the middle of the night. I was sleeping with my mother. And they told me to get dressed fast. And they took me away.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, let's take a break and change tapes, and we'll find out where they took you.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.
